llms.txt

theme.json

Every Outpost theme requires a theme.json file at the root of the theme directory. It provides the display name, version, author, and description shown in the admin theme browser.

Purpose

theme.json is purely informational. It does not configure routing, fields, collections, or any runtime behavior. Outpost reads it only to populate the theme browser in the admin panel — the name, screenshot, description, author, and version badge that appear on each theme card.

Because it has no functional effect on how templates are compiled or how pages are served, it is safe to update theme.json at any time without clearing caches or restarting anything.

Complete example

{
  "name": "My Theme",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "author": "Your Name",
  "description": "A clean theme for personal blogs and portfolios.",
  "screenshot": "screenshot.png"
}

Field reference

Field Type Required Purpose
name string Yes Display name shown in the admin theme browser. Does not need to match the directory slug.
version string Yes Semantic version string. Displayed as a badge on the theme card. Increment this when you make changes.
author string Yes Theme creator name. Shown in the theme details panel.
description string Yes A short summary of the theme's purpose and style. Shown beneath the theme name in the browser.
screenshot string No Filename (or relative path) of a preview image within the theme directory. PNG or JPG. Displayed as the theme card thumbnail.

The screenshot field

If screenshot is present and the file exists, it is served from the theme's directory and displayed as the theme card's preview image in the admin browser. The path is relative to the theme root:

{
  "screenshot": "screenshot.png"
}

This assumes the file lives at outpost/themes/my-theme/screenshot.png. You can also place it in a subdirectory:

{
  "screenshot": "assets/preview.jpg"
}

If the screenshot key is omitted entirely, or if the file cannot be found, the theme card displays a neutral placeholder graphic. This does not affect the theme's functionality — it is cosmetic only.

For best results, use a screenshot that is approximately 1200 × 900 pixels (4:3 ratio). The admin crops and scales it to fit the card thumbnail.

Tip — Take your screenshot after you have added real content and styled the theme fully. A screenshot of a blank, unstyled site is not useful for identifying the theme at a glance.

Versioning conventions

Outpost does not enforce any versioning scheme — the version field is a plain string. However, following semantic versioning (MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH) is recommended:

Change typeVersion bumpExample
Breaking layout or field name change MAJOR 1.0.0 → 2.0.0
New template or partial added MINOR 1.0.0 → 1.1.0
Bug fix, style tweak, copy change PATCH 1.0.0 → 1.0.1

Keeping the version up to date is especially valuable when distributing themes or using the Builder sync workflow — it gives you a quick way to see at a glance whether a deployed theme is current.

Validation

Outpost does not perform strict schema validation on theme.json. Any valid JSON file is accepted. However, if the file is missing or contains invalid JSON, the theme will not appear in the admin browser and Outpost will log a warning. Always confirm your JSON is valid before deploying.

Warning — If theme.json is missing entirely, the theme directory will not appear in Admin → Themes. The theme still exists on disk but Outpost will not recognize it. Always include theme.json even if it is minimal.

Minimal valid theme.json

If you are building a quick prototype and do not care about the admin display details yet, this is the minimum that will be recognized:

{
  "name": "Prototype",
  "version": "0.1.0",
  "author": "Me",
  "description": "Work in progress."
}

You can always expand it later. The screenshot field can be added at any time.

The directory slug vs. the name field

It is important to understand that the theme's slug (used internally, in the database, and in asset URLs) comes from the directory name, not from the name field in theme.json. The name field is display-only.

For example, a theme in outpost/themes/coastal-portfolio/ has the slug coastal-portfolio, regardless of whether theme.json says "name": "Coastal Portfolio" or "name": "My Theme". The slug is what Outpost stores in the settings table as active_theme, and it is what appears in asset paths like /outpost/themes/coastal-portfolio/assets/style.css.

Note — Renaming a theme directory changes its slug and breaks all references to that slug in the database (active theme setting, any hardcoded asset paths in templates). If you need to rename, update the active_theme setting in the database and update asset paths in your templates after the rename.
